title: downsampleForContainer returns an invalid Downsample cast when ratio|0 overflows to 0 (NaN or Infinity input)
description: If containerW or containerH is passed as Infinity or NaN (both pass the `<= 0` guard), `Math.min(imageW/containerW, imageH/containerH)` yields NaN. `NaN | 0` = 0, and `Math.clz32(0)` = 32, so `31 - 32 = -1`. Then `1 << Math.min(-1, 3)` = `1 << -1` = `-2147483648` in JS (bitwise ops use 32-bit signed). The `as Downsample` cast silently lies and a value of -2147483648 is returned to callers who expect `1|2|4|8`. Any downstream consumer passing this to the WASM downsample field will get an unexpected codec error or silent wrong-factor decode.

title: applyDecodePolicy silently propagates an invalid caller-supplied downsample value (e.g. 3, 5, 7) into DecodeOptions
description: DecodeOptions.downsample is typed `1 | 2 | 4 | 8` but applyDecodePolicy does not validate `base.downsample` before accepting it. A caller passing `downsample: 3` (not a valid power of two) will have that value win via `base.downsample ?? p.downsample` and land in the output unchecked. Since this is a public entry point that composes caller options with policy defaults, the function is the natural validation boundary; without it the error manifests only at the WASM bridge, making the source of the bad value opaque. Same issue in applyEncodePolicy for `effort`, `groupOrder`, etc.

title: Dead `if (!p)` guards in applyDecodePolicy and applyEncodePolicy are misleading and not tested
description: Both applyDecodePolicy (line 57) and applyEncodePolicy (line 93) contain `if (!p) throw new RangeError(...)` after indexing a frozen const-satisfies object with a `DecodePolicyName`/`EncodePolicyName`-typed parameter. TypeScript guarantees `p` is always defined at these call sites; the guard is unreachable under correct typing. The problem is that isDecodePolicyName/isEncodePolicyName are the intended entry points for untyped input, and callers may skip them and cast directly. Documenting that the guard is unreachable by type, or moving validation to the `is*PolicyName` path with a shared throw, would remove the misleading implication that typed callers can violate the invariant.

title: DecodePolicyName enum and decodePolicies table do not include a 'full' or 'hq' policy, leaving callers with no policy for non-progressive full-quality viewer
description: QUESTION: The decode policy table has 'viewer' (emitEveryPass:true, target:final) and 'export' (emitEveryPass:false, target:final) but no named policy for a high-quality viewer that wants final quality without intermediate passes yet is still interactive-priority. This gap means callers building a compare-quality or print-preview UI must construct options manually, bypassing the policy layer and losing the spec-enforcement guarantees it provides. Verify whether this is intentional spec scope or a missing entry.

category: no contract testing / schema validation at boundary
title: No test covers round-trip invariant: applyDecodePolicy output is a valid DecodeOptions accepted by the scheduler/session without further transformation
description: The test suite validates the policy logic in isolation (caller-wins, gap-fill, idempotence) but no test exercises the contract that the output of applyDecodePolicy is structurally valid as a DecodeOptions accepted by jxl-session or jxl-scheduler without further coercion. A schema-validation helper (e.g. assertValidDecodeOptions) at the boundary would catch drift if EncodeOptions/DecodeOptions adds required fields that the policy output omits, or if policy values fall outside updated enum ranges.

title: Policy tables have no version or changelog mechanism; consumers cannot detect when a policy's defaults change across package versions
description: DecodePolicies and EncodePolicies are frozen but have no semantic version separate from the package version. A consumer pinning to a policy name across package updates (e.g. 'viewer') may silently receive different defaults (e.g. if 'viewer' effort or downsample changes). Exporting a POLICY_SCHEMA_VERSION constant and noting breaking default changes in CHANGELOG would let consumers detect the drift. This is especially relevant for the mlInference policy whose downsample default directly affects ML model accuracy.
